planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and give vines room to run or a trellis to climb. This variety produces small, ribbed pure-white miniature pumpkins popular for autumn display. Harvest when the fruits reach full size and the rind firms up, keeping short stems attached.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in full sun and keep the bed watered as fruit develops. When cooked, the flesh of these oblong yellow fruits separates into spaghetti-like strands. Harvest when the rind turns deep yellow and resists a fingernail, then cure briefly.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in a sunny bed and keep evenly watered. This variety makes oblong cream fruit striped in green, with sweet flesh and tender edible skin. Harvest when the background color turns creamy and the stripes deepen; it stores for a shorter time than thick-skinned squash.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil once frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in a sunny bed and keep evenly watered. This acorn type bears colorful fruit mottled in cream, green, and orange with sweet, mild flesh. Harvest when the rind hardens and the colors set, then store in a cool, dry place.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and give vines room to spread. This variety colors up early and evenly, producing round golden-orange pumpkins good for carving. Harvest when the rind is fully colored and hard, leaving the stem attached.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and keep vines watered through fruit set. This heirloom pie pumpkin has orange skin covered in fine golden netting and smooth, sweet flesh. Harvest when the rind hardens and the netting stands out, then cure before baking.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and let vines spread in full sun. This variety is valued for carving, cooking, and its hull-less seeds for roasting. Harvest when the round orange fruit is fully colored and hard-skinned, leaving a sturdy stem.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and keep vines fed and watered. This New England heirloom bears elongated fruit that can be picked while still green and will finish ripening to orange in storage. Harvest once the ground spot turns orange, then let the fruit cure indoors.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and allow the vines plenty of room to run. This variety produces large, flattened, deeply ribbed blue-gray pumpkins with sweet orange flesh. Harvest when the rind hardens and the color sets, leaving a stub of stem attached.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last frost; tolerates heat well.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and give the vigorous heat-tolerant vines wide space to sprawl. This crookneck heirloom bears large, curved fruit striped green and white with mild flesh often used in pies. Harvest when the rind is hard and fully colored, then cure for storage.

planting.note: Direct sow once nights stay reliably above the fifties.

directions: Plant a few seeds per hill an inch deep and thin to the strongest two once true leaves appear. This old award winner rewards steady moisture and full sun with uniform, rounded fruit. Harvest at roughly eight inches for the sweetest, seedless-tasting slices.

planting.note: A heat-loving Southern variety best sown into fully warm ground.

directions: Sow an inch deep in rich, well-drained soil after the last frost. Bred for humid Southern gardens, it tolerates downy mildew better than many older sorts. Cut the tapered green fruits at six to eight inches for slicing before the seeds firm up.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil; it thrives in heat and humidity.

directions: Direct sow about an inch deep once the ground is thoroughly warm. This disease-resistant slicer stands up to mildew, scab, and anthracnose in muggy climates. Harvest the dark, straight fruits at seven to eight inches while the skin is still smooth.

planting.note: This parthenocarpic hybrid sets fruit without pollination, so covering the row is fine.

directions: Sow an inch deep and train the vigorous vines up a sturdy trellis for straight fruit. Because it fruits without bees, isolate it or grow it under cover for seedless, mild cucumbers. Pick the long, slender fruits at ten to twelve inches for bitter-free slicing.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow seeds about an inch deep in hills spaced a foot or more apart. This burpless variety is good eaten young for slicing or left a touch larger for pickling. Harvest at four to eight inches while the skin stays thin and tender.

planting.note: An early hybrid; sow once the soil warms past sixty.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep and keep moisture steady as this early hybrid comes on fast. It carries strong resistance to mildew and mosaic virus, making it dependable in warm regions. Pick the dark, slightly ridged fruits at eight inches for market-quality slices.

planting.note: Sow into warm, well-drained ground after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep and support the vigorous vines to keep the long fruit straight. This gynoecious hybrid is heavy-yielding and holds up well to common cucumber diseases. Harvest the dark green fruits at eight to nine inches for slicing.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Plant an inch deep and trellis the vines so the long fruit hangs straight. The thin, mild skin is easy to digest and needs no peeling when picked young. Harvest at ten to twelve inches while still slender for the best flavor.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil once frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow an inch deep in hills and thin to the two strongest seedlings. This classic award winner produces smooth, dark cylindrical fruit on productive vines. Harvest at about eight inches for crisp, uniform slices.

planting.note: A compact bush type well suited to containers and small beds.

directions: Sow an inch deep in a pot or bed, since the short vines need little room. Full-size fruit forms on plants only two to three feet across, ideal for patios. Pick the eight-inch cucumbers regularly to keep the compact plants bearing.

planting.note: Compact habit makes it a good container variety.

directions: Plant an inch deep in rich soil or a large pot in full sun. The short, sturdy vines carry full-length fruit without needing a trellis. Harvest the crisp eight- to eleven-inch cucumbers while the skin is still bright green.

planting.note: An award-winning compact hybrid for containers.

directions: Sow an inch deep in a container or small bed and keep evenly moist. This disease-resistant dwarf produces standard eight-inch fruit on very short vines. Pick promptly at full size to encourage continued setting through summer.

planting.note: An heirloom slicer sown into warm ground after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep and let the vines ramble or climb a support. This old variety yields long, slender, dark green fruit prized for its smooth shape. Harvest at ten to twelve inches while firm for slicing.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil for a heavy pickling crop.

directions: Sow an inch deep and keep the plants well watered as fruit sets heavily. Pick small for gherkins or slightly larger for dill spears, checking the vines daily. Frequent harvest of these blocky green fruits keeps production high through the season.

planting.note: An heirloom pickler sown after the last frost.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm soil and give the vines room to spread. This productive heirloom yields smooth, bright green fruit perfect for brining. Harvest young at two to six inches depending on whether you want cornichons or full spears.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil for a concentrated pickling harvest.

directions: Sow an inch deep and keep moisture even so the fruit stays crisp. Bred for canning, it holds firm texture whether picked tiny or at full spear size. Harvest often, taking fruit anywhere from one and a half to six inches.

planting.note: A compact pickler suited to small spaces and pots.

directions: Sow an inch deep in a container or bed since the vines stay short and bushy. This quick variety produces a flush of blocky pickling fruit early in the season. Harvest at four to five inches for whole dills or smaller for gherkins.

planting.note: An heirloom pickler direct sown after frost.

directions: Plant an inch deep in warm, rich soil and water consistently. This dependable heirloom bears deep green, black-spined fruit with square ends ideal for jars. Pick at two to six inches and harvest frequently to keep vines productive.

planting.note: A mildew-resistant pickler sown into warm ground.

directions: Sow an inch deep and keep evenly watered through fruit set. Selected for scab and mosaic resistance, it dependably yields blocky black-spined pickles. Harvest small and often for the crispest brined cucumbers.

planting.note: A bacterial-wilt-tolerant hybrid for pickling.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and water steadily as vines run. This hybrid resists bacterial wilt and sets a heavy crop of nearly seedless pickling fruit. Harvest at three to five inches for firm, crisp pickles.

planting.note: A gynoecious pickling hybrid; include a pollinator plant nearby.

directions: Sow an inch deep and keep the soil consistently moist for uniform fruit. This heavy-yielding hybrid is a favorite for its crisp, blocky pickling cucumbers. Pick daily at two to four inches to maximize the harvest.

planting.note: An early pickler bred for short northern seasons.

directions: Sow an inch deep as soon as the soil warms in cooler regions. The compact vines produce early even where summers are short. Harvest the tapered green fruit small for cornichons or at four inches for spears.

planting.note: An Asian heirloom best trellised for straight fruit.

directions: Sow an inch deep and train the vines up a tall trellis so the long fruit hangs straight. This ribbed Chinese heirloom is burpless and sweet with a thin, crisp skin. Harvest at twelve to eighteen inches while still slender.

planting.note: A Japanese-type burpless hybrid; trellis for best shape.

directions: Sow an inch deep and provide a trellis for the vigorous climbing vines. This Japanese burpless type yields long, ridged fruit with tender skin that needs no peeling. Pick at nine to twelve inches for sweet, mild slices.

planting.note: A Japanese hybrid that performs best on a trellis.

directions: Sow an inch deep and support the vines so the slender fruit grows straight. This productive Japanese hybrid bears long, dark, crisp cucumbers with a fresh flavor. Harvest at eight to ten inches while the spines are still fine.

planting.note: A vigorous Asian hybrid suited to trellis culture.

directions: Sow an inch deep and grow the vines vertically for clean, straight fruit. This heat-tolerant Asian hybrid keeps producing long slicers through summer. Harvest at ten to twelve inches while the skin remains thin and glossy.

planting.note: A Japanese-type hybrid; trellis for long straight fruit.

directions: Sow an inch deep and train the vines up netting or stakes. This Japanese hybrid resists mildew and yields slender, dark, burpless fruit. Harvest at nine to eleven inches for crisp, mild slices.

planting.note: An heirloom bearing round yellow fruit; sow after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep and let the vines climb or sprawl in full sun. The round, pale yellow fruit is mild and never bitter, best eaten fresh. Harvest when tennis-ball sized and still light yellow, before the skin turns deep gold.

planting.note: A Maine family heirloom sown into warm soil.

directions: Sow an inch deep and allow the vines to ramble or climb. This heirloom bears short, oval, creamy-yellow fruit with black spines and sweet flesh. Harvest at three to five inches while pale and tender for fresh eating or pickling.

planting.note: An ivory-fruited heirloom sown after the last frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and keep evenly watered. This heirloom produces striking ivory-white fruit with crisp, mild flesh. Harvest at six to eight inches while the skin is smooth and pale.

planting.note: A parthenocarpic type that fruits without pollination.

directions: Sow an inch deep and trellis the vines for clean, straight fruit. This Middle Eastern type has thin, smooth, spineless skin and a sweet, seedless interior. Harvest young at four to six inches for the best flavor and texture.

planting.note: Often started indoors or grown under glass for long, smooth fruit.

directions: Start seed indoors in warmth and set plants out after frost or grow them under cover. Train the vines up a support and remove side shoots for the longest, straightest fruit. Harvest the smooth, seedless English cucumbers at twelve to sixteen inches.

planting.note: An early white-fruited variety with good disease resistance.

directions: Sow an inch deep and keep steadily moist through the season. This early variety bears creamy-white fruit dotted with fine dark spines on compact vines. Harvest at three to five inches while crisp for fresh eating or light pickling.

planting.note: A parthenocarpic mini hybrid; grows well under cover.

directions: Sow an inch deep and trellis the vigorous vines for clean cocktail fruit. This all-female hybrid sets seedless mini cucumbers without pollination. Harvest at four to five inches for sweet, crunchy snacking cucumbers.

planting.note: A parthenocarpic hybrid well suited to greenhouse and early culture.

directions: Start seed in warmth and grow the vines up a string or trellis. This seedless hybrid performs well in cool, protected conditions where other types struggle. Harvest the smooth, thin-skinned fruit at seven to nine inches.

planting.note: A white-skinned slicer sown into warm soil.

directions: Sow an inch deep and keep the plants evenly watered as fruit forms. This variety produces slender, creamy-white cucumbers with crisp, sweet flesh. Harvest at four to six inches while the skin is still smooth and tender.

planting.note: A beit-alpha-style Asian type; trellis for straight fruit.

directions: Sow an inch deep and grow the vines up a trellis for clean, straight cucumbers. This thin-skinned burpless type is sweet and nearly seedless when picked young. Harvest at seven to nine inches for crisp fresh eating.

planting.note: A parthenocarpic multi-fruiting pickler that tolerates stress.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil; the small-leaved vines set fruit without bees. This stress-tolerant strain carries several fruits per node and needs no trellis. Harvest the blocky green pickles young and often for the best texture.

planting.note: A disease-resistant improvement on the Marketmore line.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm, well-drained soil and water steadily. This selection carries broad disease resistance and yields uniform dark slicers. Harvest at eight inches while firm and glossy for the best flavor.

planting.note: A smooth-skinned burpless slicer sown after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep and give the vines a trellis for straight fruit. This burpless variety has thin, mild skin and crisp flesh that is easy to digest. Harvest at six to eight inches while smooth and dark green.

planting.note: A parthenocarpic beit-alpha hybrid; grows well under cover.

directions: Sow an inch deep and trellis the vines for clean, uniform fruit. This all-female hybrid sets seedless mini cucumbers without pollination. Harvest at five to seven inches for thin-skinned, sweet slices.

planting.note: A beit-alpha-style hybrid good for both slicing and pickling.

directions: Sow an inch deep and keep evenly watered as fruit develops. This versatile hybrid can be picked small for pickles or a bit larger for salads. Harvest young and often to keep the disease-resistant vines producing.

planting.note: An old dual-purpose heirloom sown into warm ground.

directions: Sow an inch deep and let the vines climb or sprawl in full sun. This heirloom yields long, dark, warty fruit good for slicing when young or pickling when small. Harvest at ten to twelve inches for the table or earlier for the crock.

planting.note: A warm-season annual vine sown after all frost danger passes.

directions: Sow seeds about an inch deep in warm, well-drained soil, either in hills or along a trellis. Keep moisture steady once flowers appear and mulch to hold soil warmth and water. Harvest fruit while firm and green, picking often to keep the vines productive.

planting.note: Sow only after soil has warmed and all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Sow seeds about an inch deep in warm soil, planting several to a hill and thinning to the two strongest vines. Give the sprawling vines steady water while fruit sizes up, then ease off as melons ripen to concentrate sugars. Harvest when the fruit slips easily from the stem and the blossom end smells sweet.

planting.note: Direct sow once nights stay above about 55 F.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil and keep the bed evenly moist until vines run. This heirloom sets heavily netted, deep-orange fruit on vigorous vines, so mulch to keep melons off bare ground. Pick when a light tug frees the fruit at the stem and the netting turns tan.

planting.note: Sow in warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep in warm ground and thin seedlings once they have a couple of true leaves. This classic heirloom yields round, deeply ribbed melons with sweet orange flesh. Harvest at full slip when the rind between the netting shifts from green to gold.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow in warm soil.

directions: Start seeds indoors a few weeks before your frost date and harden off before setting out, or sow directly once soil is warm. This popular hybrid is prized for very sweet, aromatic orange flesh and good disease tolerance. Pick when the fruit slips cleanly and the netting is well raised and tan.

planting.note: Transplant after frost for an earlier crop in humid regions.

directions: Raise transplants indoors and move them out once the soil warms and frost has passed. This eastern-type hybrid holds up well to humidity and disease and ships without bruising, making it a market favorite. Harvest at half to full slip when the rind firms and colors to a golden tan.

planting.note: A short-season pick for northern and container gardens.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil; the compact vines suit small beds and large containers. This early heirloom ripens quickly, an advantage where summers are short. Pick the small, sweet melons at full slip when they turn golden and pull free easily.

planting.note: Direct sow after the soil is reliably warm.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in hills and thin to two vigorous vines per hill. This dependable heirloom produces round, lightly netted melons with sweet salmon flesh even in cooler summers. Harvest at full slip when the ground color warms and the stem separates cleanly.

planting.note: Sow when soil warms; tolerant of variable soils.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in warm, well-drained ground and keep young vines weeded. This large, heavily netted variety carries good fusarium tolerance and sweet orange flesh. Pick at full slip when the netting is coarse and tan and the fruit releases at a touch.

planting.note: A good northern melon; sow once soil is warm.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep after frost danger passes and thin to strong vines. Bred for cooler regions, this variety ripens reliably and offers sweet, thick orange flesh with fusarium tolerance. Harvest at full slip when the fruit smells fragrant and parts easily from the vine.

planting.note: Transplant after frost for concentrated, single-serving fruit.

directions: Start seeds indoors and set out hardened transplants once the ground is warm. This hybrid yields many small, extra-sweet personal-size melons and carries broad disease resistance. Pick at full slip when the little fruits turn tan and slip from the stem.

planting.note: Needs a long, warm season to ripen fully.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in warm soil in a spot with a long growing season. Unlike netted melons, honeydews do not slip, so watch for the smooth rind to turn creamy and the blossom end to soften slightly. Cut ripe fruit from the vine and let it rest a day or two to sweeten further.

planting.note: A Texas-bred honeydew for hot, long seasons.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and give vines room to sprawl in full sun. This honeydew offers a smooth white-green rind and sweet, pale-green flesh with good keeping quality. Harvest when the rind loses its greenish cast, turns waxy, and the blossom end gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; benefits from a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Start seeds indoors and set out after frost, giving each plant a warm, sunny position. This aromatic French melon has a smooth gray-green rind and intensely perfumed orange flesh. Because ripening happens fast, harvest as soon as the rind yellows and the fruit smells strongly sweet.

planting.note: Prefers a long, hot season; transplant after frost.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and transplant once the soil is thoroughly warm. This melon combines a netted cantaloupe-like rind with sweet, spicy green flesh and turns from green to gold as it ripens. Pick at full slip when the rind is golden and the aroma is pronounced.

planting.note: A long-season, heat-loving melon that stores well.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil in regions with a long, hot summer. This casaba develops a wrinkled golden rind and sweet white flesh, and it keeps for weeks after harvest. Cut fruit when the rind turns full yellow and the blossom end yields to a gentle press.

planting.note: Long-season Spanish melon with excellent storage.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep once soil is warm and give vines a long, sunny season. This oblong Spanish melon has a mottled green rind resembling toad skin and crisp, sweet white flesh that stores for months. Harvest when the rind background lightens and the fruit feels heavy for its size.

planting.note: Bright yellow storage melon for warm gardens.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm, well-drained soil in full sun. This canary-type melon ripens to a brilliant yellow, elongated rind over pale, mildly sweet flesh and keeps well after picking. Harvest when the rind is fully bright yellow and slightly wrinkled and the blossom end softens.

planting.note: An unusual elongated heirloom; sow in warm soil.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep after frost and let the vines ramble in full sun. This long, banana-shaped heirloom develops smooth yellow skin and fragrant salmon flesh that smells strongly of melon when ripe. Harvest when the skin is fully yellow and the fruit releases with a gentle tug.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant Ukrainian heirloom for shorter seasons.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil; this variety sets fruit reliably even where summers run cool. The round melons ripen to smooth golden-yellow skin over sweet, pale flesh. Pick at full slip when the rind is bright yellow and the fruit separates cleanly from the vine.

planting.note: An old French melon that does well in cooler summers.

directions: Start indoors and transplant after frost, or direct sow in warm soil. This centuries-old French heirloom bears small melons with gray-green rind and richly perfumed orange flesh. Harvest when the rind takes on an orange blush and the fruit smells intensely sweet.

planting.note: A dramatic ribbed heirloom; transplant after frost.

directions: Start seeds indoors and set out hardened plants once soil warms. This ornate French heirloom forms heavily lobed, warty gray-green fruit with fragrant salmon flesh. Harvest when the ground color yellows and the melon perfumes the air around it.

planting.note: A small early heirloom; sow in warm soil.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep after frost and thin to the strongest vines. This early heirloom produces small, flattened, ribbed melons often crowned with a distinctive button at the blossom end. Pick at full slip when the fruit turns light green-gold and pulls free easily.

planting.note: An early, sweet heirloom for home gardens.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in warm soil and keep the bed evenly moist as fruit forms. This small heirloom has ribbed green skin over exceptionally sweet salmon-orange flesh and ripens early. Harvest at full slip when the rind lightens toward gold and the fruit smells sweet.

planting.note: Bred in New Hampshire for short, cool seasons.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil; this early variety ripens fast in cool-summer climates. The small, oval melons carry sweet orange flesh and mature well ahead of most cantaloupes. Harvest at full slip when the netting turns tan and the fruit releases with a light tug.

planting.note: A small, showy Armenian heirloom; sow in warm soil.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep after frost and let the productive vines ramble. This small heirloom is prized for its vivid gold rind streaked with fiery orange-red stripes and its light, fragrant white flesh. Harvest when the rind colors fully and the intense floral aroma develops.

planting.note: A colorful early Indian heirloom for warm gardens.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and give the vines full sun. This striking heirloom ripens to a green rind that turns orange with dark green stripes over pale, sweet flesh. Harvest at full slip when the fruit colors up and slips easily from the vine.

planting.note: A late French storage melon; transplant after frost.

directions: Start seeds indoors and transplant after frost into a long, warm season. This golden-ball French heirloom develops smooth yellow skin over sweet, pale-green flesh and keeps well. Harvest when the rind is fully golden and the blossom end softens slightly.

planting.note: Grown like a cucumber though botanically a melon.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and trellis the vines to keep the long fruit straight. Though a melon, it is harvested young and eaten like a mild, burpless cucumber. Pick the ribbed, pale-and-dark-green fruit at twelve to eighteen inches while still slender and tender.

planting.note: A compact, early icebox type for smaller gardens.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in warm soil; the shorter vines suit compact plots. This popular icebox variety yields small, round, dark-green melons with sweet red flesh that ripen early. Harvest when the ground spot turns buttery yellow and the curly tendril near the fruit dries.

planting.note: A classic Southern variety; needs a long, warm season.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm hills and let the vigorous vines spread widely. This heirloom produces long, gray-green cylindrical melons with sweet red flesh and disease-resistant vines. Harvest when the underside ground spot turns creamy and the tendril nearest the fruit dries brown.

planting.note: A very large heirloom; needs space and a long season.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm hills, allowing generous spacing for the massive fruit. This heirloom forms very large, dark blue-green melons with sweet red flesh, some tipping the scales past forty pounds. Harvest when the ground spot turns rich yellow and the tendril by the stem dries.

planting.note: A famous speckled heirloom; sow in warm soil.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in warm soil and let the vines run. This treasured heirloom is named for the yellow spots scattered like stars with one large moon across its dark rind, and even the foliage is speckled. Harvest when the ground spot yellows and the tendril nearest the fruit withers.

planting.note: An early yellow-fleshed icebox hybrid.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil for an early, small-fruited crop. This hybrid ripens quickly to small round melons with bright yellow, notably sweet flesh. Harvest when the ground spot turns creamy and the tendril closest to the fruit dries.

planting.note: A large orange-fleshed variety for warm gardens.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in warm hills and give the vines room to sprawl. This large oblong melon has pale green striped rind and remarkably sweet, bright orange flesh. Harvest when the ground spot yellows and the curly tendril near the stem browns and dries.

planting.note: An unusual heirloom whose rind turns gold when ripe.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil; the short vines and quick maturity suit northern gardens. This small heirloom signals ripeness clearly by turning its rind from green to golden yellow. Harvest when the melons are fully gold and the tendril nearest the fruit has dried.

planting.note: A cold-adapted heirloom for short seasons.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in warm soil; this heirloom tolerates cooler climates well. It bears small round melons with pale striped rind and sweet, creamy white flesh. Harvest when the ground spot turns yellow and the tendril nearest the fruit dries.

planting.note: A heat-loving Southern heirloom for long seasons.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm hills and let the vigorous vines ramble in full heat. This old Southern heirloom makes long, light-green melons with jagged dark stripes and sweet red flesh. Harvest when the ground spot yellows and the nearest tendril dries brown.

planting.note: Bred for cool nights and short mountain seasons.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep once soil warms; this variety sets fruit even where nights stay cool. It produces round, dark-green melons with sweet red flesh and matures early. Harvest when the ground spot turns yellow and the tendril nearest the melon dries.

planting.note: A large yellow-fleshed heirloom for long seasons.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm hills and give the vines a long, hot season to finish. This large oblong heirloom carries green striped rind over sweet, deep yellow flesh. Harvest when the ground spot turns cream to yellow and the curly tendril at the stem dries.

planting.note: Grown for cooking and preserves, not fresh eating.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in warm soil; this drought-tolerant relative of watermelon thrives in heat. Its firm, white flesh is inedible raw and is instead candied or cooked into preserves and pickles. Harvest the hard, round, green-mottled fruit once the rind toughens and the vines begin to dry back.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil once nights stay reliably above the fifties.

directions: Sow soaked seed an inch deep and train the vigorous vines up a strong trellis so the curved necks hang and shape freely. Keep the plants well watered and fed until the swan-necked fruits reach full size. Leave gourds hanging until the stems brown and the mottled green shells cure hard, then dry them fully indoors before crafting.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost when the ground is warm to the touch.

directions: Sow soaked seed an inch deep and grow the vines on a tall overhead trellis so the long handles form straight as the fruit dangles. Water steadily and give the plants a full frost-free season to size up. Harvest once the stems dry and the shells turn tan and lightweight, then cure indoors until the seeds rattle.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after frost; a long season is needed to harden the large shells.

directions: Give this sprawling vine plenty of room and a heavy-duty support, sowing soaked seed an inch deep in rich, warm ground. Feed and water through the season to swell the round-bellied gourds. Let the fruit ripen fully on the vine until the shell is hard and dry, then cure for several weeks before drilling entrance holes for birdhouses.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; harvest young for eating rather than curing.

directions: Sow soaked seed an inch deep and trellis the fast vines so the slender fruits hang long and straight. Keep the soil evenly moist and pick often to keep new fruit coming. Harvest the pale green gourds while young and tender at twelve to eighteen inches, when the skin is soft enough to scratch with a fingernail.

planting.note: Sow into warm, well-drained soil after all frost danger.

directions: Soak the seed overnight and sow half an inch deep, then trellis the slender vines for the long, spindle-shaped fruit. Keep the plants evenly watered and fed through the heat of summer. Cut the deeply ridged green fruit at eight to twelve inches while firm and pale, harvesting frequently to prolong the crop.

planting.note: Needs consistent warmth; sow well after the last frost.

directions: Sow soaked seed half an inch deep in warm soil and support the vines on a trellis to keep the smooth fruit clean. Water steadily and avoid letting the plants dry out during flowering. Pick the plump, pale green to whitish fruit while young and glossy, as they are milder than the darker types and quickly turn bitter if left to ripen.

planting.note: Long, hot season required; sow well after frost.

directions: Sow seed about an inch deep in warm, rich soil and let the vines run on the ground or up a sturdy trellis with fruit support. Keep the plants well fed and watered through the long season. Harvest the large fruit once the skin firms and develops its whitish, waxy bloom, which lets these melons store for many months in a cool room.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm soil; harvested young unlike its winter-melon relative.

directions: Sow seed about an inch deep in warm ground and trellis the vines to keep the fuzzy young fruit off the soil. Provide steady moisture and regular feeding for tender growth. Pick the small cylindrical fruit while the skin still carries its fine hairs, usually at six to ten inches, before it matures and toughens.

planting.note: Warm-loving; sow after frost or start indoors in short-season areas.

directions: Soak the seed and sow about an inch deep, giving the vigorous vines a tall trellis so the ridged fruit hangs long and straight. Water regularly and pick often for tender eating. Harvest the ribbed green fruit young at eight to twelve inches for the kitchen, or leave a few to dry on the vine into fibrous sponges.

planting.note: Start indoors weeks before the last frost since sponges need a very long season.

directions: Start soaked seed indoors in warmth, then set plants out after frost onto a strong trellis for the long, cylindrical fruit. Feed and water generously all season. Pick young fruit under six inches for cooking, but for bath sponges leave the gourds until the skin turns brown and papery, then peel, wash out the seeds, and dry the fiber.

planting.note: Grown from tubers; needs both male and female plants for fruit set.

directions: Plant dormant tubers a few inches deep in warm, well-drained soil, including male tubers so the female vines can set fruit. Give the slender vines a trellis and keep them evenly watered during active growth. Harvest the small, spiny green fruits while young and tender; the tubers go dormant and resprout each warm season.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after frost; a heat-loving short-vined cucurbit.

directions: Sow soaked seed about an inch deep in warm, well-drained soil where the compact vines can sprawl. Water steadily and keep the beds weeded while the plants establish. Harvest the small, round, pale green fruits while young and tender at golf-ball to tennis-ball size, before the seeds harden.

planting.note: Sow after frost; long-vined and needs a full warm season to mature fruit.

directions: Sow seed about an inch deep in warm, rich soil and give the sprawling vines plenty of room to run. Keep the plants watered through the season for good fruit set. Harvest the round, green-and-white-striped fruit when the shell hardens; the white, stringy flesh keeps for many months and is used for candied sweets.

planting.note: Sow after frost; the large round shells need a long season to cure.

directions: Sow soaked seed an inch deep in warm, rich soil and let the heavy vines ramble with room to spread. Water and feed steadily to swell the big, round fruit. Leave the gourds on the vine until the stems dry and the shells harden, then cure them for several weeks until light and hollow-sounding for carving or bowls.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; grown on the ground so fruit flattens on one side.

directions: Sow soaked seed an inch deep and let the vines run along the ground so the broad fruit rests and flattens into a basket-friendly shape. Water and feed through the long season. Allow the gourds to ripen fully on the vine until the shells are hard and tan, then cure and dry them thoroughly before cutting and cleaning for bowls or baskets.

planting.note: Sow after frost; grown for colorful small autumn gourds.

directions: Sow seed about an inch deep in warm soil and let the vines sprawl or climb a fence for cleaner fruit. Water regularly and watch for squash bugs and cucumber beetles common to this species. Let the small warted and striped gourds fully harden on the vine, then cut with a bit of stem and cure them in a dry, airy place for autumn display.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata 'Early Jersey Wakefield'

planting.note: An heirloom early type set out before the last frost for a late spring harvest.

directions: Start seed indoors six weeks before setting out and plant seedlings deeply up to the first leaves. This compact conical variety tolerates close spacing, so give it steady moisture to prevent the pointed heads from splitting. Harvest the firm cone-shaped heads while still tight in early summer.

planting.note: A slightly later, larger companion to Early Jersey Wakefield.

directions: Raise transplants indoors and set them out into rich, firm soil once frost danger eases. Its broad pointed heads run a week later than Early Jersey but grow noticeably heavier, so keep the bed weed-free and well watered. Cut the semi-pointed heads once they firm up fully.

planting.note: A dependable small early round cabbage for spring planting.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant sturdy seedlings after hardening off in cool spring weather. The tidy globe-shaped heads mature early and stay compact, making them a good fit for smaller beds and closer spacing. Harvest the round green heads when firm and about six inches across.

planting.note: A classic uniform ballhead heirloom for spring or fall.

directions: Begin seed indoors and move hardened transplants to fertile ground with a short stem below the head. Its uniform round heads sit on a compact plant with few outer leaves, so plant fairly close and water evenly. Cut the solid globe heads once they weigh three to four pounds.

planting.note: A hybrid prized for very solid, split-resistant heads.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant well-rooted seedlings into firm, fertile soil. This compact hybrid forms exceptionally dense heads that resist splitting and hold well in the garden past maturity. Harvest the rock-solid three-pound heads when tight, and they can wait a while without bursting.

planting.note: A late keeper best timed to head up in cool autumn weather.

directions: Sow for a fall crop and transplant into deep, rich soil with generous spacing for the large frame. These dense round heads are slow to mature but store for months in a cool cellar. Cut after a light frost has sweetened the leaves for the best keeping quality.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata 'Late Flat Dutch'

planting.note: A big heirloom drumhead grown for fall harvest and storage.

directions: Start seed early and give transplants very wide spacing to accommodate the sprawling outer leaves. These flattened heads can reach ten to fifteen pounds, so feed heavily and keep the soil consistently moist. Harvest the broad flat heads in autumn once solid, and they hold well in cool storage.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata 'Premium Late Flat Dutch'

planting.note: A refined strain of the flat Dutch drumhead for late crops.

directions: Raise transplants and set them into deeply worked, fertile soil with wide spacing. The large flattened blue-green heads store exceptionally well through winter when kept cool and humid. Cut when the heads feel dense and heavy in mid to late autumn.

planting.note: A hardy German drumhead heirloom well suited to fall and kraut.

directions: Sow for a fall crop and transplant into rich soil with room for the large spreading leaves. This cold-tough drumhead forms broad flat heads valued for sauerkraut and long keeping. Harvest after cool weather sets in, cutting the heads once they turn firm and solid.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata 'Glory of Enkhuizen'

planting.note: A dependable Dutch ballhead heirloom for main-season crops.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant sturdy plants into fertile ground once hardened. It produces large, solid round heads that are excellent fresh or for kraut and hold well in the row. Harvest when the globes are firm and full, typically in late summer.

planting.note: A hybrid main-season type with good field holding.

directions: Begin seed indoors and set out well-grown transplants into firm, fertile beds. This hybrid makes uniform blue-green globes that resist splitting and hold their quality in the garden. Harvest the dense heads when solid, from late summer into fall.

planting.note: A widely grown hybrid noted for disease resistance.

directions: Raise transplants indoors and set out hardened seedlings into rich soil. This hybrid offers strong resistance to yellows and tipburn while forming tight, uniform round heads. Cut the firm blue-green globes once they reach three to five pounds.

planting.note: A dependable hybrid fresh-market cabbage.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant robust seedlings into well-prepared soil. The dense blue-green heads sit on a compact frame and resist splitting when left in the field. Harvest when solid and heavy for their size, usually in mid-season.

planting.note: A hybrid selected for sweet, tender leaves for fresh use.

directions: Start seed indoors and move hardened transplants into fertile, moist soil. The heads form crisp, sweet, thin leaves that are especially good raw in slaws and salads. Cut the round green heads while firm and use them fresh soon after harvest.

planting.note: A quick early round cabbage for spring planting.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant seedlings early into cool, fertile soil. This fast maturing variety forms sweet, tender round heads and stands close spacing well. Harvest the compact green heads promptly once firm to keep them from splitting.

planting.note: An early hybrid that stands well without splitting.

directions: Begin seed indoors and set out hardened transplants in early spring. This early hybrid forms tender, sweet round heads that hold in the field longer than most fast types. Cut the firm two to three pound heads for the freshest quality.

planting.note: A modern hybrid pointed cabbage good for tight spacing.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant seedlings into fertile soil, spacing them close for its upright habit. The neat pointed heads have sweet, tender leaves and few wasteful outer wraps. Harvest the cone-shaped heads while firm and use fresh in salads or slaws.

planting.note: A small single-serving hybrid for close planting.

directions: Sow indoors and set out transplants at close spacing to size the mini heads. This compact hybrid makes dense, softball-sized heads ideal for small households and containers. Harvest the tight one to two pound heads once firm, and they hold well without bursting.

planting.note: A very early mini cabbage bred for containers and tight beds.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant into rich soil or a large container. This quick mini hybrid forms small, tender heads on a very compact plant that resists splitting. Harvest the one-pound heads as soon as they firm up for the sweetest flavor.

planting.note: A popular early red heirloom for spring and fall.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant hardened seedlings into fertile ground. This reliable red variety forms compact purple-red globes that resist splitting and store well. Harvest the firm heads once they color up fully and feel solid.

planting.note: A hybrid red cabbage with good storage life.

directions: Start seed indoors and set out transplants into rich, firm soil. This hybrid produces deep red, dense heads that hold in the field and keep for months in storage. Cut the solid heads when fully colored and heavy for their size.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata 'Mammoth Red Rock'

planting.note: A large late red heirloom for fall harvest.

directions: Raise transplants early and give them wide spacing for the large frame. This heirloom forms big deep-red heads of five to eight pounds prized for slaws and pickling. Harvest in autumn once the heads are solid and richly colored throughout.

planting.note: An early red type that matures fast in short seasons.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant seedlings into fertile soil after hardening off. This early open-pollinated red matures quickly and suits short-season gardens and close spacing. Harvest the compact oval red heads once firm, before summer heat sets in.

planting.note: A hybrid red cabbage that holds well in the garden.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant into rich, firm soil. This hybrid forms uniform round red heads that resist splitting and bolt slowly in warm spells. Cut the solid three to four pound heads once fully colored.

planting.note: A hybrid red cabbage with deep uniform color.

directions: Sow indoors and set out hardened transplants into fertile beds. This hybrid delivers dense, deeply colored red heads with good field holding and storage. Harvest the firm heads once they reach full size and rich color.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. sabauda 'Savoy King'

planting.note: A vigorous savoy hybrid with crinkled tender leaves.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant vigorous seedlings into rich, moist soil. This savoy hybrid forms semi-flat heads of deeply crinkled, tender leaves that cook and eat sweetly. Harvest the firm heads in cool weather when the savoyed leaves are well developed.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. sabauda 'Savoy Perfection'

planting.note: A classic savoy with heavily crinkled blue-green leaves.

directions: Sow indoors and set out hardened transplants into fertile ground with ample room. This savoy forms large, flattened heads of thick, curled leaves that sweeten after frost. Cut the heads once firm in cool autumn weather for the best texture and flavor.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. sabauda 'Vertus'

planting.note: A hardy late French savoy for autumn and winter harvest.

directions: Start seed for a fall crop and transplant into deep, rich soil. This cold-hardy French savoy forms flattened heads of dark, blistered leaves that stand into winter. Harvest as needed through cold weather, which sweetens the crinkled leaves.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. sabauda 'Melissa'

planting.note: A round savoy hybrid with dense, curled heads.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant sturdy seedlings into fertile, moist soil. This savoy hybrid forms rounded, solid heads of deeply savoyed leaves with fine flavor. Harvest the firm heads in cool weather, and they hold well in the garden and storage.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. sabauda 'Alcosa'

planting.note: An early compact savoy hybrid for close spacing.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant seedlings into rich soil at moderate spacing. This early savoy makes small, tender crinkled heads that mature faster than most savoys. Harvest the compact two to three pound heads once firm and well savoyed.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. sabauda 'Wirosa'

planting.note: A very hardy savoy hybrid for overwintering.

directions: Sow for a fall crop and transplant into deep, fertile soil. This exceptionally cold-hardy savoy forms solid heads of dark crinkled leaves that stand through hard frosts. Harvest through late autumn and early winter, cutting heads as needed.

planting.note: Sow in early summer for a very hardy winter harvest.

directions: Start seed in late spring or early summer and transplant into firm, rich soil for a long grow. This hardy overwintering type forms flat, blue-green semi-savoyed heads flushed with purple in cold weather. Harvest through winter as needed, since the heads withstand hard frost and snow.

planting.note: A hybrid storage cabbage with long keeping quality.

directions: Sow for a fall crop and transplant into deep, fertile soil. This hybrid forms dense, heavy heads bred to hold in the field and store for many months. Harvest after cool weather firms the heads, and cure them for long winter keeping.

planting.note: A hybrid bred specifically for long-term winter storage.

directions: Start seed for a fall crop and transplant into rich, firm ground. This hybrid produces very dense round heads that can keep six months or more in cold storage. Harvest the solid heads in late fall and store them cool and humid.

planting.note: One of the earliest hybrid cabbages for spring.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant seedlings early into fertile, cool soil. This very early hybrid forms small, tender round heads quickly and suits close spacing for baby cabbage. Harvest the compact heads as soon as they firm up to keep them from splitting.

planting.note: A hardy hybrid that stands in the field into hard frost.

directions: Start seed for a fall crop and transplant into deep, fertile soil. This tough hybrid holds its dense heads in the garden through repeated frosts and stores well afterward. Harvest the firm heads in late autumn, or leave them standing until needed.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. sabauda 'Savoy Ace'

planting.note: A widely grown savoy hybrid with good heat tolerance.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant sturdy seedlings into rich, moist soil. This savoy hybrid forms solid rounded heads of crinkled leaves and tolerates warmth better than many savoys. Harvest the firm heads once the savoyed leaves are fully developed.

planting.note: A hardy hybrid for late fall and early winter harvest.

directions: Start seed in summer and transplant into firm, fertile soil for a long autumn grow. This cold-hardy hybrid forms flattened semi-savoyed heads that flush deep purple after frost. Harvest through late fall and early winter, as cold sweetens the leaves.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or midsummer for a fall crop that sweetens after frost.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter to half inch deep and thin seedlings so plants stand roughly a foot apart. Keep soil evenly moist and side-dress with nitrogen once plants are established for tender, blistered blue-green straps. Begin picking outer leaves once they reach eight to ten inches, and the flavor turns sweeter after the first frosts.

planting.note: Plant in spring or late summer; the low compact habit resists cold well.

directions: Drop seed a quarter inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil and thin to a hand's width apart. This short, spreading plant holds its finely curled blue-green leaves close to the ground, so mulch to keep them clean. Harvest outer leaves as needed and the plant will keep producing through hard frost.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out hardened transplants a few weeks before the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant vigorous seedlings a foot or more apart in rich soil. This hardy F1 hybrid throws tall, deeply ruffled blue-green leaves that stand up to snow and rebound in spring. Pick lower leaves regularly and the central stem will keep pushing new growth from the top.

planting.note: Set out transplants in spring or late summer; color deepens in cold weather.

directions: Transplant seedlings about a foot apart into fertile ground and water steadily. The tightly curled purple-red foliage grows tall and stately, intensifying to deep burgundy as nights turn cold. Harvest outer leaves through fall and winter, or leave a few plants for a striking ornamental accent.

planting.note: Best set out as transplants for uniform, productive stands.

directions: Grow from transplants spaced roughly a foot apart in rich, moisture-retentive soil. This uniform hybrid produces dense, finely curled dark green leaves on a medium-tall frame that holds well in the field. Pick outer leaves as they size up, and the plant tolerates cold late into the season.

planting.note: Transplant in spring or midsummer for continuous cut-and-come-again harvests.

directions: Set hardened transplants about a foot apart in fertile soil and keep them well fed. This tall green hybrid produces heavily curled leaves on strong stems that resist lodging. Strip the lower leaves regularly for a long picking season that stretches from summer into winter.

planting.note: Compact habit suits containers and small beds; sow spring or fall.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to about a foot apart, or grow in roomy containers. This award-winning dwarf hybrid has short internodes and nearly stemless, ruffled dark green leaves that stay tender. Snap off outer leaves as needed and the tidy plants keep refilling from the center.

planting.note: A hardy old European strain; sow in spring or summer for autumn and winter use.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings to stand a foot apart in firm, fertile ground. This low, hardy strain bears tightly curled green leaves that shrug off frost and snow. Take outer leaves through the cold months, leaving the crown to regrow.

planting.note: Color deepens with cold; grow for both eating and ornament.

directions: Transplant seedlings roughly a foot apart in well-drained, fertile soil. The finely curled leaves emerge green-purple and turn a deep scarlet-purple as temperatures drop. Harvest young leaves for salads or let plants mature for cooking greens through the cold season.

planting.note: An Italian heirloom-type flat kale; sow spring or late summer.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin to about a foot apart in rich soil. Plants form upright rosettes of long, narrow, heavily savoyed blue-green leaves. Pick from the bottom up once leaves reach eight inches, and flavor mellows and sweetens after frost.

planting.note: Fast, cold-hardy and slow to bolt; good for early spring and fall.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings to a foot apart in fertile ground. This quick, vigorous variety produces smooth to lightly frilled dark green leaves and tolerates both cold and short-season conditions. Begin cutting outer leaves early, since the plants hold well without bolting.

planting.note: A vigorous curled hybrid; set out transplants for uniform bunches.

directions: Transplant seedlings about a foot apart in rich, well-drained soil and keep evenly watered. This sturdy hybrid produces thick, deeply curled blue-green leaves on strong petioles that bunch cleanly. Harvest full-size outer leaves and the plant will continue producing through cold weather.

planting.note: A tender oil-seed-type kale; excellent for baby leaf and overwintering.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to a hand's width for baby leaf or a foot apart for full heads. The flat, oak-leaf-shaped blue-green leaves have purple stems and veins that redden in the cold. Cut tender young leaves for salad or larger ones for cooking; the plant is among the sweetest and most cold-hardy kales.

planting.note: A very hardy white-stemmed selection; ideal for winter harvests.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings for the desired leaf size. This selection carries the frilled, oak-leaf foliage of Red Russian but with white stems and even greater cold tolerance. Pick outer leaves through hard freezes, and expect the sweetest flavor after repeated frosts.

planting.note: An extremely hardy, low-growing kale for tough winters.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in fertile ground and thin plants to about a foot apart. This spreading, low kale bears broad, blue-green leaves with lightly frilled edges and shrugs off severe cold. Harvest outer leaves as needed and the productive crown regrows quickly in mild spells.

planting.note: More heat-tolerant than most kales; a staple of Portuguese caldo verde.

directions: Set transplants about eighteen inches to two feet apart in rich, well-drained soil. This robust tronchuda type forms a loose rosette of broad blue-green leaves with thick, tender white ribs. Harvest the outer leaves and midribs for soups and braises; plants tolerate summer heat better than curly kales.

planting.note: Vigorous Portuguese cabbage-kale that tolerates heat and cold alike.

directions: Transplant seedlings a foot and a half to two feet apart in fertile soil with steady moisture. This tronchuda selection makes large, smooth, blue-green leaves with prominent pale ribs and a mild flavor. Strip lower leaves as the plant grows tall, using both the blade and the succulent midrib.

planting.note: A Southern heirloom tolerant of heat, poor soil, and light frost.

directions: Sow seed a quarter to half inch deep and thin plants to about two feet apart, since they grow large. This old cabbage-collard bears loose rosettes of broad, cabbagey blue-green leaves and takes heat as well as frost. Pick lower leaves from the bottom up and the plant keeps producing for months.

planting.note: Compact, slow to bolt, and very cold-hardy for spring or fall.

directions: Direct sow shallowly and thin to about eighteen inches to two feet apart in fertile soil. This dependable dwarf collard forms compact plants with smooth, deep green leaves that resist bolting and heat. Harvest outer leaves once they are hand-sized, and flavor improves after autumn frosts.

planting.note: An improved Vates type with strong bolt resistance and cold hardiness.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep and thin plants to roughly two feet apart in rich ground. This Vates-derived selection is compact and especially slow to bolt, holding its dark green, waxy leaves in both spring and fall. Cut lower leaves as needed, and the sturdy plants stand well into cold weather.

planting.note: A heading-type Southern heirloom that forms a loose cabbage-like center.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to about two feet apart in fertile, well-drained soil. Unlike most collards, this heirloom forms a loose central head of tender, mild leaves above its outer rosette. Harvest the outer leaves through the season and cut the buttery center head when it firms up.

planting.note: A vigorous hybrid that regrows quickly after picking and resists bolting.

directions: Transplant seedlings about two feet apart in rich soil and keep them well watered. This productive F1 hybrid holds up in summer heat and rebounds fast when leaves are stripped for repeated harvests. Pick the smooth, dark green upper leaves regularly and the plant resists bolting and yellowing.

planting.note: A fast hybrid bred for easy top-bunching harvest of young leaves.

directions: Set transplants roughly two feet apart in fertile ground and water consistently. This quick hybrid produces a flush of tender, slightly savoyed leaves at the top that are easy to cut and bunch. Harvest young leaves often for the best texture, and the plant quickly grows a new crown.

planting.note: A uniform hybrid with good cold tolerance for fall and overwintering.

directions: Transplant seedlings about two feet apart in rich, moisture-retentive soil. This uniform hybrid produces heavy yields of smooth, blue-green leaves and holds its quality in cold weather. Harvest lower leaves as they mature, and the plants overwinter well in mild-winter regions.

planting.note: An old glossy-leaved heirloom that resists cabbage worms and heat.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin plants to about two feet apart in fertile soil. This heirloom's waxy, bright-green glossy leaves shed water and shrug off many leaf-eating pests. Harvest outer leaves through the season; the variety tolerates heat well and sweetens after frost.

planting.note: A vigorous hybrid with strong disease resistance and even growth.

directions: Transplant seedlings about two feet apart into rich, well-drained soil and water steadily. This robust hybrid produces uniform, dark green leaves and stands up to common collard diseases. Pick lower leaves as they reach full size, and the plants remain productive well into cold weather.

planting.note: A tender Eastern Carolina heirloom prized for its mild, sweet flavor.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin plants to about two feet apart in fertile ground. This regional heirloom carries pale yellow-green, tender leaves that cook up milder and sweeter than standard collards. Harvest outer leaves as needed; the variety is best in cool fall and winter weather.

planting.note: A blue-leaved Appalachian heirloom, very cold-hardy and slow-growing.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep and thin to about two feet apart in fertile soil. This tall heirloom produces distinctly blue-tinged leaves on stout stalks and is extremely cold-hardy. Pick lower leaves through fall and winter; the flavor grows notably sweeter after hard frosts.

planting.note: A cold-selected landrace bred for reliable overwintering in the mid-Atlantic.

directions: Sow in late summer for fall and winter, thinning plants to about two feet apart. This diverse landrace was selected for extreme cold hardiness and yields tender leaves through freezing weather. Harvest outer leaves as needed, and the hardiest plants can be left to overwinter and reseed.

planting.note: The classic tall Southern heirloom that thrives in heat and poor soils.

directions: Sow seed a quarter to half inch deep and thin plants to about two feet apart. This vigorous heirloom grows tall with a woody main stalk and broad, waxy blue-green leaves that tolerate summer heat and light frost. Harvest lower leaves from the bottom up and the plant produces continuously for months.

planting.note: Start seedlings indoors and set out a few weeks before the last spring frost, or sow direct in mid-summer for a fall crop.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter inch deep and transplant sturdy seedlings into rich, firm soil once they have several true leaves. Keep plants evenly watered and feed with a balanced fertilizer to encourage a tight central head. Cut the main head while the buds are still firm and closed, then let the plant push out smaller side shoots for weeks of continued picking.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant heirloom best timed as a fall crop, set out in late summer to mature in cool weather.

directions: Plant transplants deeply, up to the first set of leaves, in a spot with steady moisture and fertile soil. This dependable open-pollinated variety shrugs off light frost and colors up best as nights cool in autumn. Harvest the domed blue-green head before the individual buds begin to loosen or show yellow.

planting.note: An early Italian heirloom that produces a small main head followed by heavy side-shoot growth.

directions: Start seeds indoors and move hardened seedlings into loose, fertile ground once frost danger eases. Because this variety ripens unevenly, plan for a modest first head and generous cut-and-come-again shoots. Snip the central head early and keep harvesting the smaller florets that follow for many weeks.

planting.note: A vigorous, heat-tolerant hybrid suited to both spring and fall plantings.

directions: Set transplants into well-drained, compost-enriched soil and mulch to keep roots cool. This adaptable hybrid holds its quality across a wide season and resists premature flowering in warm spells. Cut the smooth, tightly beaded dome when it reaches full size but before any yellow petals appear.

planting.note: A widely grown organic-friendly hybrid that performs in both cool spring and fall windows.

directions: Transplant robust seedlings into firm, fertile soil and water consistently to prevent stress. This reliable variety forms a large, dome-shaped head and rewards you with plentiful side shoots afterward. Harvest the main head while the buds stay compact and dark green.

planting.note: A cold-loving hybrid prized for fall and overwintered crops in mild climates.

directions: Set transplants deep into rich soil and keep them well fed through the cool growing months. Marathon thrives in low temperatures and produces a very heavy, tightly beaded head. Cut the central dome when firm, then collect the steady flush of side shoots that follows.

planting.note: An open-pollinated variety valued for large heads and a long side-shoot season.

directions: Grow seedlings indoors and transplant into fertile ground once they are sturdy. This variety yields a broad main head and continues to send up side florets over an extended period. Harvest heads while the buds are tight and green, cutting the stem at a slant to shed rain.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant hybrid especially good for fall harvests and disease resistance.

directions: Transplant hardened seedlings into rich, moisture-retentive soil for the fall season. Arcadia stands up well to cold and common brassica diseases while forming a heavy, domed head. Cut the head before the beads swell or loosen, ideally after a light frost sweetens the flavor.

planting.note: An extra-early hybrid ideal for the first spring picking or quick fall turnaround.

directions: Start seeds early indoors and move them out under protection for a jump on the season. This fast, cold-vigorous hybrid produces a blue-green dome well ahead of most varieties. Harvest promptly once the head fills out, since early types can bolt quickly in warming weather.

planting.note: A heat-tolerant hybrid that holds well through warm spells where others bolt.

directions: Transplant into fertile soil and keep well watered as temperatures rise. Sun King tolerates heat better than most, making it a good choice for late-spring plantings that finish in summer. Cut the large, well-domed head while buds remain tight, then enjoy a strong flush of side shoots.

planting.note: A dependable main-season hybrid with strong disease resistance and heavy side shoots.

directions: Set seedlings into firm, compost-rich soil and maintain even moisture throughout growth. Diplomat resists downy mildew and produces both a solid central head and abundant secondary florets. Harvest the main head at full size before any buds begin to yellow.

planting.note: A compact, heat-tolerant hybrid well suited to container growing and warm regions.

directions: Grow this space-saving hybrid in a large container or a snug garden spacing with steady feeding. Destiny handles heat gracefully and forms neat, medium heads on tidy plants. Cut the head while firm, and keep the plant watered for a follow-up of small side shoots.

planting.note: An early, adaptable hybrid that produces large heads and prolific side shoots.

directions: Transplant seedlings early into rich soil and keep them growing without check. Packman matures quickly and follows its big first head with a long run of secondary florets. Harvest the central head while beads are tight, then continue picking side shoots for several weeks.

planting.note: An award-winning hybrid known for very large, uniform, tight heads.

directions: Set out sturdy transplants into fertile, well-drained soil and feed regularly for maximum size. Premium Crop reliably forms a broad, dense head with little tendency to develop hollow stems. Cut the head at full size before the buds swell, using a sharp knife on a slanted stem.

planting.note: An open-pollinated variety noted for a small central head and heavy side shoots.

directions: Start seedlings indoors and transplant into fertile, evenly moist soil. Nutri-Bud emphasizes side-shoot production, so harvest the main head early to trigger a long parade of smaller florets. Pick the tender shoots regularly while their buds stay closed to keep new ones coming.

planting.note: An open-pollinated variety bred for cool maritime climates and good side-shoot yield.

directions: Transplant seedlings into rich, cool soil and keep moisture steady through the growing season. Umpqua does well in mild, damp regions and produces a good central head with reliable follow-on shoots. Harvest the head while the beads remain tight and blue-green.

planting.note: A vigorous hybrid producing domed heads with strong disease tolerance in spring or fall.

directions: Set out transplants into firm, fertile soil and water evenly for smooth head development. Emerald Crown offers dependable performance across changing weather and good resistance to common leaf diseases. Cut the well-domed head before buds loosen, then gather the side shoots that follow.

planting.note: A heat- and stress-tolerant hybrid that performs on lighter soils and in warm spells.

directions: Transplant into well-drained soil where Gypsy's vigorous roots handle heat and lighter ground with ease. This adaptable hybrid resists downy mildew and forms a domed, medium-green head. Harvest when the head is full but still tight, then let side shoots extend the picking.

planting.note: A heat-tolerant summer hybrid that resists bolting and beading in warm conditions.

directions: Set transplants into rich soil and mulch well to buffer summer heat around the roots. Imperial is bred to hold quality through warm weather that would cause other broccoli to loosen or flower. Harvest the smooth, high-domed head while beads stay tight and uniform.

planting.note: A hybrid selected for humid eastern climates with strong heat tolerance and uniformity.

directions: Transplant into fertile soil and keep evenly watered through humid, warm summers. Eastern Magic is adapted to muggy conditions and forms large, dependable heads with a nice dome. Cut the head at full size before the beads begin to swell or show color.

scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. italica 'Early Purple Sprouting'

planting.note: An early overwintering strain that yields its first spears at the tail end of winter.

directions: Sow in summer and set transplants out with plenty of room to develop a strong frame before cold arrives. This early-maturing strain delivers purple spears sooner than most, bridging the late-winter hungry gap. Harvest spears young and often to keep the plant productive for many weeks.

planting.note: A very early purple sprouting type that can crop in late autumn or overwinter for spring.

directions: Sow in late spring or early summer for the earliest possible sprouting broccoli harvest. Rudolph produces large, thick purple spears well ahead of standard overwintering types. Cut the central spear first to prompt many side spears, and keep picking while buds stay tight.
